---
title: "Why I changed my LinkedIn headshot to a Lego cartoon, and why I changed it back"
description: "Identity, low stakes, and the moment you realise you're allowed to look like the thing you've become."
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-05-27"
categories: [career, mres]
draft: true
---

When I started the MRes in biomedicine I swapped my LinkedIn photo for a Lego cartoon of myself. A few minutes ago I changed it back to a real headshot, two years later, with the dissertation nearly done. A few people have asked me about it. Here's the honest answer.

The professional headshot stopped being honest the moment I stopped being a software exec. I was about to be a Master's student at fifty, sitting in undergraduate lectures, trying to remember what the Krebs cycle was. The corporate photo would have lied about what I was doing. But I wasn't ready to claim "biologist" either — I had nothing yet to back it up. The Lego avatar was the only image that told the truth: this person is in transit and unwilling to pretend otherwise.

Cartoon avatars also lower the stakes. They tell anyone who clicks: I know this is weird, I know I'm fifty and starting over, I'm not pretending to belong yet. Useful when you're walking into a department full of people half your age, half of whom assume you're someone's dad.

There was a control thing too, I think. LinkedIn rewards a particular kind of polished professional self-image; refusing it was a small act of agency at a moment when most of the other decisions about my life felt uncomfortable. Once being a comp-bio researcher started to feel like a thing I actually was, the agency stopped needing the avatar.

What changed in two years is that I now have something to back the word "biologist" with. Lab-meeting talks, a dissertation in flight, the first proper version of a computational platelet model. The cartoon was honest two years ago; it'd be false modesty now.

So: in for the transition, out for the consolidation. If you're in the middle of a career switch and your LinkedIn photo feels false either way, a Lego avatar is a perfectly sensible interim solution. Take it down when you've got something to put up that isn't.
